design_philosophy.core_loop: Observe planetary systems, adjust environmental variables, watch life evolve and civilizations develop across billions of simulated years until sentient life achieves exodus or planet dies
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing planetary conditions (atmosphere, temperature, water, continental drift) within narrow ranges to support increasingly complex life forms while managing random disasters and unintended consequences
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Act as a godlike planetary architect and evolutionary guide, sculpting entire worlds and civilizations across geological timescales, guided by scientific principles (Gaia hypothesis)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: SimEarth embeds James Lovelock's Gaia hypothesis as core mechanic rather than backdrop—planet is self-regulating system where life and environment coevolve. Game rejects traditional 'win state' in favor of open-ended experimentation or extinction. Species phylogeny is egalitarian (sapient molluscs equally valid as sapient humans), rejecting anthropocentric evolution narrative. Embraces emergence and inscrutability: 'species may thrive or die out for no apparent reason,' treating mystery as feature not bug

difficulty.checkpointing: Planetary development stages can be restored and repeated; ten billion year lifespan per planet before Sun becomes red giant
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Selectable difficulty levels control energy budget—lowest difficulty provides unlimited energy units for terraforming and environmental modifications; higher difficulties constrain interventions by energy scarcity. Random Planet mode eliminates goals entirely, making difficulty purely self-imposed

progression.upgrade_system: Lifeforms progress through evolutionary stages (prokaryotes → eukaryotes → multicellular animals → sentient species → civilizations). Civilization development tracked through technological ages (culminating in Nanotech Age). Environmental tools unlock through gameplay (oxygen generators, monoliths, terraforming devices)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Scenarios unlock sequentially: Aquarium and Cambrian Earth (managing Earth evolution), 1990 Earth (contemporary management), Mars/Venus/Ice Planet/Dune (terraforming challenges), Earth 2XXX (civilization rescue). Daisy World mode available as alternative. Machine life Easter egg triggered by destroying nanotech city with nuclear explosion
replayability_hooks: Random Planet mode with procedural planetary generation
Eight distinct scenarios with different objectives and starting conditions
Emergent evolutionary outcomes—same initial conditions may produce wildly different species and civilizations
Multiple difficulty levels changing energy constraints
Experimentation after mass extinction events allows testing new ecosystem combinations

world_building_techniques: Scientific advisory (James Lovelock co-designed Gaia mechanics)
Procedural emergence: player witnesses planetary history unfold through ecosystem development, climate shifts, civilization arcs
Scenario framing provides context (Aquarium, Cambrian Earth, terraforming missions, apocalyptic rescue)
Tool descriptions embed sci-fi references (monolith from 2001: A Space Odyssey)
Disaster system creates narrative tension (hurricanes, volcanic eruptions, nuclear war, nuclear winter, tsunamis, plague)
Easter egg machine life suggests post-apocalyptic transhumanism

faction_indicators: Civilization technological progression (tracked across ages, culminating in Nanotech Age)
Species taxonomy diversity (radiates, cetaceans, fish, birds, molluscs, custom evolved species)
danger_signals: Nuclear war triggered by fuel shortage in nuclear power-dependent civilizations
Global warming causing ice cap melt and ocean boiling
Nuclear winter from nuclear weapon use
Plague and disease correlated with population density
environmental_storytelling: Atmospheric composition displays (oxygen, nitrogen, CO2 percentages to three decimals) reflect planetary habitability
Sea level changes visible from ice cap melting
Volcanic dust effects on global temperature (visible temperature shifts)
Continental drift visualization showing plate tectonics
Civilization cities and infrastructure density indicating development stage
